Motion To Stay Arbitrations By Abd Insurance And Financial Services, Inc., Richard Weingart, And Reed Freyermuth, Jr.; Memorandum Of Points And Authorities In Support Thereof
Off Calendar May-26-2022 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
PLAINTIFFS ABD INSURANCE AND FINANCIAL SERVICES INC., A, RICHARD WEINGART and REED FREYERMUTH'S Motion To Stay Arbitrations.

APPEARANCES:
Michael D. Weil, Esq. (415.442.1107; michael.weil@morganlewis.com) of Morgan, Lewis & Bockius LLP for Plaintiff.
Robert Milligan, Esq. ((310.277.7200; rmilligan@seyfarth.com) and Marcus Mintz, Esq. (312.460.5000; mmintz@Seyfarth.com) of Seyfarth Shaw LLP for Defendants, VIA ZOOM VIDEOCONFERENCE.

ARGUED (as fully reported in the reporter's transcript). The Court having reviewed the pleadings and having heard oral argument adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

Plaintiffs ABD Insurance and Financial Services, Inc. d.b.a. Newfront ("Newfront"), Richard Weingart ("Weingart"), and Reed Freyermuth, Jr. ("Freyermuth") (collectively "Plaintiffs") Motion to Stay Arbitrations is denied.

Code of Civil Procedure section 1281.2(c) permits this Court to "stay arbitration pending the outcome of the court action" if there is a pending court action which arises "out of the same transaction or series of related transactions and there is a possibility of conflicting rulings on a common issue of law or fact." Plaintiffs re-assert arguments found in the Opposition to the Petition to Compel Arbitration and Stay Court Action.

"The petitioner bears the burden of proving the existence of a valid arbitration agreement by the preponderance of the evidence, and a party opposing the petition bears the burden of proving by a preponderance of the evidence any fact necessary to its defense. In these summary proceedings, the trial court sits as a trier of fact, weighing all the affidavits, declarations, and other documentary evidence, as well as oral testimony received at the court's discretion, to reach a final determination." (Giuliano v. Inland Empire Personnel, Inc. (2007) 149 Cal.App.4th 1276, 1284.) In deciding whether to compel arbitration, judges identify the controversy and then decide whether it is within the scope of the arbitration provisions. (Titolo v. Cano (2007) 157 Cal.App.4th 310, 316.) "'Doubts as to whether an arbitration clause applies to a particular dispute are to be resolved in favor of sending the parties to arbitration. The court should order them to arbitrate unless it is clear that the arbitration clause cannot be interpreted to cover the dispute."' (California Correctional Peace Officers Ass'n v. State (2006) 142 Cal.App.4th 198, 205.)

As stated in the Court's tentative granting HUB's petition, the Court finds that the arbitrable arbitration provision in the SPA (1) applies to the claims asserted by Plaintiffs, (2) is not inconsistent, (3) delegates issues of arbitrability under JAMS Rules to the arbitrator, and (4) Newfront, as a nonsignatory, claims are stayed pending the completion of arbitration. Where a court orders arbitration, it shall stay the pending action until arbitration is had in accordance with the order to arbitrate or another earlier time, and the stay may be with respect to an issue that is severable. (CCP sec. 1281.4; Cruz v. PacifiCare Health Systems, Inc. (2003) 30 Cal.4th 303, 320; Twentieth Century Fox Film Corp. v. Sup. Ct. (2000) 79 Cal.App.4th 188, 192; Heritage Provider Network, Inc. v. Sup. Ct. (2008) 158 Cal.App.4th 1146, 1152, 1154 n. 12.) As such, this action is stayed and the Motion to Stay the Arbitration is denied.

Order is signed.

Judge: Richard B. Ulmer Jr., Clerk: S. Le, Court Reporter: Jane Hong, CSR# 11975 (909-831-2644; csrjane@hotmail.com), Reported. =(302/RBU)
